p.s: for a reason, deliberately
"on purpose. Comment: This idiom is usually used when someone does something wrong or unfair.
to perform badly, to make a mistake (also: to mess up, to slip up)
to goof up.
all the time, from the beginning (without change)
"all along.
really, actually (also: in fact)
"as a matter of fact.
to insist on, to demand; to defend, to support
"to stand up for.
to work very hard (sometimes too hard) to do something
"to knock oneself out. Comment: A reflexive pronoun must divide the idiom.
to succeed in doing something difficult (S); to exit to the side of a highway
to pull off.
occasionally, sometimes (also: now and again, at times, from time to time, off and on, once in a while)
"now and then. Comment: Both now and then and once in a while can be preceded by the adjective every. Another idiom with the same meaning and form is every so often.
to spend money freely, to live luxuriously
to live it up.
"to be very busy, to have too much to do (also: to be up to one's ears); to be beyond one's ability to understand"
"to be over one's head.
to appear, to arrive; to be found or located (also for the second definition: to turn up)
"to show up.
to locate information in a directory, dictionary, book, etc. (S)
"to look up.
useless, without the desired result
"in vain.
to distinguish between (also: to pick apart, to tell from) (S)
"to tell apart.
to resemble a parent or close relative (for physical appearance only, also: to look like)
to take after.
to happen
"to come about.
to associate or work well with; to succeed or manage in doing (also: to get on with someone or something)
"to get along with someone (something).
many
quite a few.
temporarily (also: for now)
"for the time being.
to pay the bill at a hotel or motel and then leave; to investigate, to examine (S)
to check out. Comment: The adjective form check-out derives from this idiom.
get information about, to determine (S)
"to find out. Comment: This idiom is separable only when a pronoun is used, as in the second example.
not feeling well, sick
under the weather.
to hope to have good results, to hope that nothing bad will happen
"to keep one's fingers crossed. Comment: This idiom reflects the way people cross their fingers to hope for good luck.
to visit or stop somewhere briefly in order to do something
"to stop by.
to be finished, to end (also: to be through)
to be over. Comment: This idiom is used for activities and events.
"to be unpopular or no longer in fashion; to be away from one's work or home"
"to be out.
to explode, to go flat (for tires); to extinguish by blowing (S)
"to blow out.
it seems likely, reasonable, or typical
it figures. Comment: This idiom is either followed by a that-clause or by no other part of grammar.
to be of importance (to), to affect
"to make a difference (to someone). Comment: This idiom is often used with adjectives to show the degree of importance.
with the inside facing the outside
inside out.
to be victimized or cheated
"to be had.
risky, uncertain until the end
touch and go.
to withstand use or wear; to fail to appear for a date or social engagement (S)
"to stand up.
to do without rush, not to hurry
"to take one's (my, his, your, etc.) time. Comment: This idiom is often used in the imperative form. (See the first example)
to economize, to save money
to cut corners.
to receive credit for the value of an old item towards the purchase of a new item (S)
to trade in. Comment: This idiom is used to form the noun trade-in.
to discard, to dispose of (S)
"to throw away.
to complete a form (S)

to fill out. Comment: This idiom is very similar to the first definition above. To fill in refers to completing various parts of a form, while to fill out refers to completing a form as one whole item.
to delay, to make late (S); to remain high in quality
to hold up.
having contact
"be in touch (with).
to be sensible or reasonable
"to make sense.
to win or defeat by gaining an advantage over someone
"to get the better of.
to play music that one has heard but never read (S); to proceed without plan, to do spontaneously (S)
to play by ear. Comment: The pronoun it is often used with the second definition.
to rip into small pieces (S)
to tear up.
to stop working for the rest of the day/night
"to call it a day/night.
to expect or anticipate with pleasure
"to look forward to. Comment: This idiom can be followed by a regular noun or a gerund.
to operate accurately (for watches and clocks)
"to keep time. Comment: This idiom is usually used with adjectives such as good and perfect.
to burn slowly, but completely (usually said of candles); to destroy completely by fire (S)
to burn down.
to start traveling toward a place (also: to set off, to head out); to arrange or display neatly (also: to lay out) (S)
to set out.
to eliminate, to remove; to discard, to throw away
to get rid of.
to lag, to fail to keep up (also: to get behind)
to fall behind.
not to move (S)
"to hold still.
clearly stated, definite, apparent
"clear-cut.
to match, to compare well in color or design; to date, to accompany (also: to go out with)
to go with. Comment: For the first definition, adverbs such as well and poorly are often used.
alert, cautious
"on one's toes. Comment: This idiom is usually used with the verbs stay and keep.
to inform, to give background information to (also: to clue in) (S)
"to fill (someone) in. Comment: This idiom is often followed by the preposition on and a noun phrase containing the pertinent information.
"to be popular or fashionable; to be available at one's work or home"
"to be in.
to give attention or time to (also: to attend to, to see to)
"to see about.
"to block or obstruct; not to be helpful, to cause inconvenience (for both, also: to get in the/one's way)"
"to be in (the/one's) way.
to regain consciousness; to equal, to amount to
to come to.
to begin to do or study, to undertake (S); to occupy space, time, or energy (S)
"to take up.
to return (S)
"to get back.
to provide a person with another opportunity or chance (S); not to expect too much work from (S); not to expect someone to believe (S)
"to give (someone) a break. Comment: Command forms are most common with this idiom. For the third definition, the pronoun me must be used.
impossible, not feasible
"out of the question.
to look at and listen to someone while they are speaking, to concentrate
"to pay attention (to).
to stir or shake well (S); to confuse, to bewilder (S)
"to mix up. Comment: For the second definition, the passive forms to be mixed up or to get mixed up are often used.
to enjoy oneself
to have a good time.
regarding, concerning (also: as to)
"as for.
this one time, for only one time
for once.
to determine to be unacceptable, to refuse to consider
"to draw the line at.
to reveal or tell what you know, to hint
"to let on.
to avoid discussing directly, to evade the issue
to beat around the bush.
to get very angry and do something hastily
"to go off the deep end.
to buy the complete stock of (S)
to buy up.
partially deaf, not able to hear well
"hard of hearing.
to maintain the same speed or rate as
"to keep up with.
to become better, worse, etc.
"to get better (worse, etc.).
to go for a walk, stroll, hike, etc.
"to take a walk, stroll, hike, etc.. Comment: A stroll involves slow, easy walking; a hike involves serious, strenuous walking.
to motivate to behave or act in a certain way (S)
"to make (someone) tick. Comment: This idiom is used within a what-clause.
to make unconscious (S); to impress or attract greatly (S)
"to knock out. Comment: This idiom can be made into the noun form knockout for both definitions.
to exercise; to develop, to devise (a plan) (S)
"to work out.
not to exist anymore; to be in the process of disappearing
to die out.
to depart quickly (also: to storm out); to act suddenly
to burst out. Comment: For the second definition, this idiom is usually followed by a gerund form such as laughing, crying, singing, etc.
to compromise with someone
to meet (someone) halfway.
by memorizing
by heart.
continuously, constantly (also: day after day; for longer periods of time, year in and year out and year after year)
day in and day out.
to reach a decision, to decide finally
"to make up one's (my, his, your, etc.) mind.
to be extensive, to discuss much material
"to cover ground. Comment: Forms such as a lot of, too much, too little are used before the noun ground.
to use something until it has no value or worth anymore, to make useless through wear (S)
"to wear out.
to recognize (S)
to know by sight. Comment: This idiom is used when the person has been seen previously but is not known personally. The person must be used to separate the idiom.
to grasp, to grip with the hands
to take hold of.
to disappear gradually
"to wear off.
not to enter, not allow to enter (S)
"to keep out.
to exchange greetings by clasping hands
to shake hands.
to become popular or widespread; to understand, to appreciate a joke
to catch on. Comment: This idiom is often used with the preposition to for the second definition.
to distribute (also: to hand out) (S); to lose consciousness
to pass out. Comment: The verbal idiom to hand out can be made into the noun handout to refer to items that are distributed in a class or meeting.
finally, absolutely
"once and for all.
to cause anger (S); to create (trouble or difficulty) (S)
"to stir up.
to remain at home, not to go out
to stay in. Comment: An idiom with the opposite meaning is to stay out.
to stop functioning

to break down. Comment: Compare this idiom with to burn out in Lesson 12. To burn out means that electrical equipment becomes hot from overuse and stops functioning. To break down means that something stops functioning mechanically, whether from overuse or not.
alone, without assistance
by oneself.
to remember, not to forget (S) (also: to bear in mind)
"to keep in mind.
to make shorter, to interrupt (S)
"to cut short.
to face, to overlook
to look out on.
to happen; to resume, to continue (also: to keep on)
"to go on.
to communicate with, to contact
"to get in touch with.
to watch as a spectator, to observe
to look on.
to employ, to hire (S); to accept responsibility for, to undertake
to take on.
extinguish, to cause to stop functioning (S)
"to put out. Comment: To put out has the same meaning as to turn off (Lesson 1) for a light fixture.
to rear, to raise from childhood (S); to mention, to raise an issue, to introduce a topic (S)
to bring up.
to consult someone about an important matter (S)
"to take up with. Comment: The important matter follows the verb take, while the person consulted follows with.
To question or interrogate someone intensely and relentlessly (about something).
grill (someone) (about something)
to intend to, to act purposefully to
to set out to do something.
to admire, to respect greatly
to look up to.
to distribute; to become exhausted or depleted (also: to run out)
"to give out. Comment: The first definition has the same meaning as the second definition of to pass out in Lesson 19.
hurried, rushed (also: in a rush)
"in a hurry.
approximately, almost; somewhat, to a certain degree
"more or less.
to rise from a sitting or lying position (also: to get up)
to stand up.
"to display one's ability in order to attract attention (S); to let others see, to expose to public view (S)"
"to show off. Comment: This idiom can form the noun showoff for the first definition.
to create by drawing, such as a map (S); to prepare documents or legal papers (S)
"to draw up.
the final event in a series of unacceptable actions
"the last straw. Comment: This idiom is always used with the definite article the.
to ask for a response from; to visit (also: to drop in on)
"to call on.
before the time necessary to do something
in time to (do something).
survive or exist without something (also: to go without)
"to do without.
to remove from an elevated place (S); to write what is said, to note
to take down.
to be able to focus or concentrate on (also: to get with it)
"to be with it. Comment: To be with it in the negative has the same meaning as to feel out of it. The related form to get with it is used in commands.
to destroy completely by fire (S); to make angry or very annoyed (S) (also to tick off)
"to burn up. Comment: To burn up and to burn down (previous idiom) share the same definition but also have different definitions.
to close for a period of time (S); to be quiet, to stop talking
"to shut up. Comment: The second definition of this idiom is impolite in formal situations.
"not to think clearly, to lose one's self-control"
"to lose one's head.
to drive or go backwards (S); to defend, to support (S); to return to a previous thought
"to back up.
"don't be concerned about it; ignore what was just said"
"never mind.
to try to find, to search for
"to look for. Comment: An adverb phrase such as all over can be put between the verb and preposition, as in the second example, however, the idiom cannot be separated by a noun or pronoun.
must (also: have to)
"have got to.
to mislead by joking or tricking (S)
"to put (someone) on. Comment: This idiom is usually used in a continuous tense form. A noun object must divide the idiom.
very much, greatly
in the worst way.
to place on oneself (usually said of clothes) (S)
to put on.
to have enough money to enjoy a comfortable life, to be rich (also: to be well-to-do)
to be well-off.
very quickly, rapidly
"in no time. Comment: This idiom can be used with the idiom at all to add emphasis to the certainty of the statement.
to want revenge on, to feel hostile towards (also: to hold a grudge against)
to have it in for.
to any degree (also: in the least)
"at all. Comment: This idiom is used with the negative to add emphasis to a statement.
to happen to (a missing object or person)
"to become of. Comment: This idiom is always used in a clause beginning with what.
to perceive or understand as (S)
"to take for. Comment: This idiom is usually used when someone is mistakenly perceived. A noun or pronoun must separate the idiom.
"to take someone's place temporarily, to substitute for; to protect someone by lying or deceiving"
"to cover for.
to leave without permission; to escape
"to run away.
to wait until late at night without going to bed
"to wait up for.
"to avoid one's responsibility, to quit"
"to cop out. Comment: This idiom is an informal version of the second definition to back out (lesson 29). The noun form copout means an excuse for avoiding responsibility.
to have the necessary skills or talent for
to be cut out for. Comment: The idiom is most often used in the negative or in questions.
to be sure, to ascertain (also: to make certain (of))
"to make sure.
to exhaust the supply of, not to have more of
"to run out of.
to become familiar with routine procedures at work or school
"to learn the ropes.
to stop functioning because of overuse; to make tired from too much work (S)
to burn out.
to meet or fulfill a missed obligation at a later time (S); to create, to invent (an idea) (S); to apply cosmetics to (S); to comprise, to be composed of
"to make up. Comment: Note that all of the definitions are separable except the last one.
to examine with the purpose of determining condition (also: to check on)
to check up on. Comment: This idiom has the related noun form checkup.
to stop trying; to stop a bad habit (S); to surrender (S)
"to give up.
not willing to accept the ideas of others (the opposite of narrow minded is broad-minded)
narrow-minded.
gradually, slowly (also: step by step)
"little by little.
to resolve differences with
to make up with. Comment: This idiom is used for differences of opinion between friends and lovers.
to investigate, to examine carefully (also: to check into)
to look into.
with the upper side turned toward the lower side
upside down.
to solve, to find a solution (S); to understand (S)
to figure out.
to be considering, to be thinking (S)
"to have in mind.
to fulfill (a standard or promise)
"to live up to.
to release, to produce
to give off.
to form a line; to arrange to have, to manage to obtain (S)
to line up.
certainly, definitely, naturally (also: of course); using any possible way or method
"by all means.
to say good-bye upon departure by train, airplane, bus, etc. (also: to send off) (S)
to see off. Comment: A noun or pronoun must divide the idiom.
regardless of
"no matter. Comment: This idiom is a shortened form of it doesn't matter. It is followed by a question word such as how, where, when, who, etc.
rehearsal, practice session
dry run.
to send a request through the normal way
"to go through channels. Comment: This idiom can be used with the adjective proper.
to do something with enthusiasm and thoroughness
"to go to town.
to destroy by making flat, to demolish (S)
to tear down.
"I don't know (also: beats me)"
"search me. Comment: This idiom is used informally, usually as a command form.
to sell all items (S); to arrange for the sale of a company or business (S)
"to sell out.
"to be useful, to suit one's needs or requirements"
"to serve (the/one's) purpose.
to erect or construct quickly (S); to vomit (S)
to throw up.
to discuss or consider a situation with others (S)
"to talk over.
"to slacken, to lessen in intensity; to relax or ease one's effort (also: related idiom: to take it easy)"
"to let up.
to increase slowly, to make stronger gradually (S)
to build up.
to feel superior to, to think of someone as less important
to look down on.
to undergo, to experience; to consume, to use (also: to use up)
"to go through. Comment: The first definition is used when someone is having some hardship or difficulty.
to expire, to be finished
"to be up. Comment: This idiom is used only with the word time as the subject.
to surprise, to amaze, to astonish (S)
"to take by surprise. Comment: A noun or pronoun usually divides this idiom.
to pity, to feel compassion for (also: to take pity on)
"to feel sorry for.
to move or face in the opposite direction (S); to completely change the condition of (S)
to turn around.
uncomfortable or worried in a situation
ill at ease.
to be responsible for deciding; to be doing as a regular activity
"to be up to. Comment: The second definition is most often used in a question as a form of greeting.
to be appreciated or accepted
"to go over. Comment: This idiom is usually followed by the adverb well. (In Lesson 6 this idiom has the meaning to review, as in the second sentence of the second example below.)
to occur, to happen according to plan
"to take place.
from this time into the future
"from now on.
to remind constantly, to nag
"to keep after.
to keep or maintain a record of; to remember the location of
to keep track of.
to begin to love
to fall in love. Comment: This idiom is used with the expression at first sight to indicate a sudden interest in love.
to neglect a duty (S); to ignore a situation (S)
to let slide.
to become sick with a cold of the nose or throat
to catch cold.
to annoy or disturb (also: to bug)
"to get on one's nerves.
to meet or find unexpectedly (also: to run across); to be perceived or judged as (also: to come off)
"to come across.
to arise from sleep, to awaken (S)
"to wake up. Comment: Compare wake up and get up (Lesson 1) as used in the first example.
exactly at the correct time, punctually
on time.
to remove (usually said of clothes) (S)
to take off.
to diminish gradually in time or distance
to fade away.
predictable, known beforehand; boring
cut and dried.
prefer to (also: would just as soon (do something) as (do something else))
would rather (do something).
to appear promising or optimistic, to be improving
to be looking up. Comment: This idiom is used in a continuous tense, very often with the subject things.
to be greatly affected by a strong feeling (S)
to be carried away (by, with). Comment: This idiom can also be used with get instead of be.
to serve in a store or restaurant
"to wait on someone.
not be able to convince someone

"to waste one's breath. Comment: This idiom is used when someone is wasting time trying to convince another person. The idiom to save one's breath is related and means not to waste effort trying to convince someone.
to laugh at, to joke about
"to make fun of.
to travel a sufficient distance at a reasonable speed
"to make good time. Comment: The adjective excellent can also be used.
to be doing something; to be planning or plotting something, scheming
"to be up to (something). Comment: The first definition usually takes the form of a question.
to confuse, to scramble (S); to cause problems in (S)
"to screw up.
to postpone (S)
"to put off.
to start or cause to function (also: to switch on) (S)
"to turn on.
to be involved in, to participate in (also: to be in on)
"to take part in.
considering everything
"all in all.
to accept as a responsibility or task
"to bite off. Comment: This idiom is often used when one accepts more responsibility than one can handle alone. It is usually used in the form to bite off more than one can chew.
to have the possibility of accomplishing something
"to stand a chance. Comment: This idiom is often used with an adjective such as good or excellent. It also occurs in the negative, sometimes with the adjective much.
to show or introduce (to the public) (S); to make available (S)
to bring out.
without doubt (also: for certain)
"for sure.
divided into two equal parts
"fifty-fifty.
available, nearby
on hand. Comment: This idiom is often followed by in case.
to bear a human being or animal
"to give birth to.
to answer in a rude manner, to speak disrespectfully
"to talk back to.
compromise, cooperation between people
give and take.
to maintain contact with (also: to stay in touch with)
to keep in touch with. Comment: This idiom should be compared with to get in touch with in Lesson 9.
excellent, superb
first-rate.
to increase (also: to drive up); to be constructed, to be erected
to go up. Comment: The second definition is the same as the one for to put up in Lesson 19, except that to go up is not used with a noun object.
to go on a journey, to travel
"to take a trip (to).
to recover from an illness; to accept a loss or sorrow
"to get over.
to stop attending; to withdraw from
to drop out of. Comment: This idiom can be made into the noun form dropout.
to fail, to result badly
"to go wrong.
to extend, to keep for a longer time (S)
"to hold over.
not having contact; not having knowledge of
out of touch with.
and certainly not (also: not to mention, to say nothing of)
"let alone. Comment: Let alone is used after negative forms. The example that follows let alone is much less possible than the example that precedes let alone.
"to alter one's decision or opinion"
"to change one's (my, his, your, etc.) mind.
to empty, to tidy by removing (S); to steal, to rob (S); to buy or purchase all of something (S)
"to clean out.
to be certain to do something, to be sure to do something
"to be bound to do something. Comment: This idiom is used when the occurrence of an event seems inevitable or unavoidable.
to construct, to erect (S); to lift, to raise upwards (S)
to put up.
to lose an opportunity, to fail in some undertaking
"to miss the boat.
suddenly, without warning (also: all at once)
"all of a sudden.
to communicate with, to make someone understand (also: to break through to)
"to get through to. Comment: This idiom has the meaning of to make someone ""catch on"" (Lesson 29, eighth idiom, the first definition)
to grasp tightly or firmly; to wait, to be patient
"to hold on. Comment: The second definition is often used when someone is talking on the telephone.
direct, personal; directly, personally (written without hyphens)
"face-to-face. Comment: This idiom can be used both as an adjective (the first definition) and as an adverb (the second definition).
a situation involving a narrow escape from danger (also: close shave)
close call.
to avoid punishment for
"to get away with.
to remove from sight, to put in the proper place (S)
to put away.
to accompany a person out of a house, building, etc. (S)
"to see out. Comment: A noun or pronoun must again divide the idiom.
to represent, to signify; to tolerate
"to stand for. Comment: The second definition is usually used in a negative sense. The meaning is the same as to put up with in Lesson 19.
nervous, anxious; upset, irritable
"on edge.
to become available to others
to be up for grabs. Comment: This idiom is used when something is highly desirable to many other people.
to reduce in brightness or volume (S); to reject, to refuse (S)
"to turn down.
repeatedly (also: time after time, time and again)
"over and over (again).
"it's no surprise that, not surprisingly"
"no wonder. Comment: This idiom derives from reducing it is no wonder that…
gradually to prepare something for use that is new and stiff (S); to interrupt (for the second definition, also: to cut in)
"to break in.
"to review something in order to refresh one's memory"
"to brush up on something.
to approach (also: to come up to, to walk up to, to run up to, to drive up to, etc.)
"to go up to. Comment: The related forms have the same meaning, but the type of movement is different.
incidentally
"by the way. Comment: This idiom is used when someone thinks of something further in the course of a conversation.
to originate from
"to come from. Comment: This idiom is commonly used in discussion of one's home town, state, or country.
to agree, to concur
"to see eye to eye (on something/with somebody).
to waste time, to be idle
to goof off.
to place upside down (S); to flip, to turn upside down; to pass or give control to someone (S)
to turn over.
"to purchase a business or company (S); to purchase all of a person's shares or stock (S)"
to buy out. Comment: This idiom is similar in meaning to take over in Lesson 23.
to become used to, to become adjusted to
"to get used (to something). Comment: This idiom describes the process of change that allows someone to be used to a situation, action, or habit.
to criticize, to complain about something
"to find fault with.
to do, to succeed, to progress
"to make out.
to have as an interest, such as a sport or hobby (also: to go for, to be into, to get into)
"to go in for.
to seek revenge, to retaliate
"to get even with. Comment: This idiom is similar in meaning to to have it in for in Lesson 27.
to be unsatisfactory, to be improper, to be wrong
"to be the matter. Comment: In a question, this idiom is used with what or something. In an answer, something or nothing is usually used.
to become unable or afraid to do something
to get cold feet. Comment: This idiom is usually used in the case of an important or dangerous action.
to cancel by marking with a horizontal line (S)
"to cross out.
to conceal, to hide (S)
"to cover up. Comment: This idiom is used for events which are potentially embarrassing to one's reputation, as well as against the law. The noun coverup can be formed.
to explode, to sound as an alarm; to leave suddenly without explanation
"to go off.
to assume control or responsibility for (S); to do or perform again (S)
"to take over. Comment: The meaning of the second definition is almost the same as do over in Lesson 11. Also for the second definition, a noun or pronoun must divide the idiom.
to cancel (S)
to call off.
"to arrange matters the way one wants (especially when someone else doesn't want the same way) (also: to get one's way)"
"to have one's way.
basically, fundamentally
"at heart. Comment: This idiom is used to describe the true character of a person.
to remain awake, not to go to bed
"to stay up.
to fail at what one used to do well
"to lose one's touch.
to get free, to escape
to get away.
to manage, to cope
"to make do with. Comment: This idiom is used when a person must accept a substitute that is not the most suitable.
to make happier, to feel less sad (S)
"to cheer up.
to test, to use during a trial period (S)
to try out.
not in working condition
"out of order.
to have, to possess
"have got.
in the beginning, originally
at first.
to energize, to make more active (also: to pick up) (S)
to liven up.
to delay making a decision on (S)
"to leave open.
to watch, to supervise, to protect (also: to take care of, to keep an eye on)
"to look after.
to treat severely, to discipline; to go faster, to work more quickly
"to step on. Comment: For the second definition, the idiom is followed by the pronoun it.
to have a (good or bad) opinion of
"to think of. Comment: This idiom is often used in the negative or with adjectives such as much and highly.
"to lift from the floor, table, etc., with one's fingers (S)"
to pick up.
to place clothes on a hook or hanger (S); to replace the receiver on the phone at the end of a conversation (S)
to hang up.
in general, in most ways (also: by and large)
on the whole.
to be in a more favorable condition or situation
"to be better off. Comment: The opposite of this idiom is to be worse off.
to find guilty of a crime or offense (S) (also: to hang on)
"to pin on. Comment: This idiom is divided by a noun phrase containing the crime or offense. The accused person is mentioned after the preposition on.
to lose, or cause to lose, all moisture (S); to be depleted
to dry up.
should, ought to, be advisable to
"had better (do something). Comment: This idiom is most often used in contracted form (I'd better).
to fall in love quickly; to be fooled or tricked by
to fall for.
to wear clothes to check the style or fit before buying (S)
"to try on.
to become reality, to prove to be correct
"to come true.
to drive a vehicle out of a parking space (S); to withdraw support, to fail to fulfill a promise or obligation
to back out.
to decrease, to lessen in strength
to die down.
"to gather, to collect (information) (S); to gain control of one's emotions (S)"
to pull together. Comment: A reflexive pronoun must be used for the second definition.
the entire day, week, month, year
"all (day, week, month, year) long.
to waste time (also: to screw around); to joke, not to be serious
"to fool around.
to receive news or information from
"to hear from. Comment: To hear from is used for receiving a letter, telephone call, etc., from a person or organization.
to deserve, to receive a just punishment (also: to bring upon)
"to ask for.
to be responsible for an office while others are gone
"to mind the store.
to consider a fact while evaluating a situation (S)
"to take something into account. Comment: Again, a noun or pronoun often follows the verb take.
"to be in another person's position, to face the same situation as another person"
"to be in (someone's) shoes.
be attentive, competent, alert
"be on the ball.
To encourage, support, or cheer for someone or something; to wish the best for someone or something in an endeavor or activity.
root for
to abstain from, stop using as a habit; to release or discharge from a job (also: related idiom: to let go) (S)
"to lay off.
to gain (pounds or weight) (S); to present, to perform (S)
"to put on.
"give one's attention to; to watch"
to look at.
to become free or loose, to escape
to break loose.
to be very different from
to be a far cry from.
to have some connection with or relationship to
to have to do with.
"to restore one's financial security, to resolve a difficult financial obligation"
to get out from under.
absolutely not (also: no way)
"not on your life. Comment: This idiom is used as a kind of exclamation by itself.
to eat at home/to eat in a restaurant
"to eat in/to eat out.
to stay or remain where one is, to wait
"to stick around. Comment: This idiom is used when someone is waiting for something to happen or for someone to arrive.
to be sufficient or adequate for everyone present; to circulate, to move from place to place
"to go around.
to fall asleep; to take to a certain location (S); to decrease (for the third definition, also: to fall off)
"to drop off.
to meet someone unexpectedly; to crash or collide into (also: to bump into)
to run into.
to accept as true, have faith in
"to believe in.
to stop a bad habit
"to kick the habit.
very inexpensive, a bargain
"a steal. Comment: This idiom is often used in an exclamation using what.
to be clear and logical
"stands to reason. Comment: This idiom is almost always used with the pronoun subject it and is followed by a that clause.
to telephone (also: to give someone a call) (S)
"to call up. Comment: To call can be used instead of to call up, as in the first example below.
to fail to materialize, not to succeed
to fall through. Comment: This idiom is usually used with the noun plan or plans as the subject.
a hopeless case, a person or situation having no hope of positive change
"a lost cause.
as is the general case, as is typical
"as usual.
to write answers in (S); to inform, to tell (S)
"to fill in. Comment: For the second definition, the idiom can be followed by the preposition on and the information that someone is told.
eventually, after a period of time
"sooner or later.
to cause to stop functioning (also: to switch off, to shut off) (S)
to turn off. Comment: Turn on and turn off, as well as their related forms, are used for things that flow, such as electricity, water, gas, etc.
to behave so as to gain favor with someone
to play up to.
to stay at a distance (from) (S); to avoid use of (also: stay away from)
"to keep away (from).
to disobey or ignore normal procedures or rules (also: to step out of line)
"to get out of line.
to shorten by cutting the ends (S); to disconnect or stop suddenly (S)
to cut off.
however, in contrast
on the other hand.
to make understandable (also: to straighten out) (S); to become sunny
"to clear up.
to retire or leave a top position, to resign
to step down.
to clarify, to explain (S)
to make clear.
not frequent, unusual, rare
few and far between.
to cause to end, to terminate in a definite manner (also: to do away with)
to put an end to.
to interest greatly, to excite (S)
to turn on. Comment: The idiom with the opposite meaning is to turn off. These idioms are used to form the nouns turnon and turnoff.
under firm control, well managed
"in hand.
to trust someone in time of need (also: to depend on)
"to count on.
"to clap one's hands in applause, to applaud (S)"
to give (someone) a big hand.
"to express one's true feelings (S)"
"to get off one's chest. Comment: This idiom is used when someone has long waited to express themselves.
ready to, just going to
"be about to do smth.
to share involvement in
"to have a voice in.
to delay, or to be delayed, in occurring (S)
to hold off. Comment: This idiom has the same meaning as to put off in Lesson 5 when a noun or pronoun is used as an object, as in the second example.
not to disturb, to stay away from (S) (also: to let alone)
to leave (someone or something) alone.
acceptable, fine; yes, okay
all right. Comment: This idiom can also be spelled alright in informal usage.
to assist, to aid, to help (also: to lend someone a hand) (S)
"to give (someone) a hand.
to become friendly with others
"to make friends (with).
eventually, after a long period of time
"in the long run. Comment: This idiom is similar in meaning to sooner or later (Lesson 1). The difference is that in the long run refers to a more extended period of time.
to have the desire to, to want to consider
"to feel like. Comment: This idiom is usually followed by a gerund (the -ing form of a verb used as a noun).
to begin to burn
"to catch fire.
to be seated (also: to take a seat)
"to sit down.
not to appreciate fully (S); to assume to be true without giving much thought (S)
"to take for granted. Comment: A noun or pronoun often follows the verb take.
to inflate, to fill with air (S); to explode, to destroy (or be destroyed) by explosion (S)
to blow up.
in order to be prepared if

"in case. Comment: When the idiom occurs at the end of the sentence (the second example), then the meaning is in order to be prepared if something happens. The ""something"" might be an accident, a delay, etc.
to provoke a response from
"to get a rise out of. Comment: This idiom is usually used when someone is teased into responding in anger or annoyance.
to manage, to have responsibility for
to be in charge (of something).
to have current knowledge of; to understand as an explanation
"to keep up with. Comment: This idiom should be compared to the meaning of to keep up with in Lesson 17.
very soon; immediately (also: at once)
"right away.
to be easily visible or noticeable (also: to stick out)
to stand out. Comment: This idiom is used for someone or something that is different from all others.
to have free time, not to have to work (also: to take time off (S))
to have (time) off. Comment: The related form (S) to take time off is used when someone makes a decision to have free time, sometimes when others might not agree with the decision.
to prevent from sleeping (S); to continue maintaining (speed, level of work, condition, etc.) (S)
"to keep up.
to invent, to create (also: to dream up)
to think up. Comment: This idiom is often used for an unusual or foolish thought.
to continue as before; to conduct, to engage in; to behave in an immature manner
"to carry on.
to repair or put back in good condition (S); to arrange a date or an engagement for another person (S)
"to fix up.
to wear formal clothes, to dress very nicely
"to dress up.
in the order of; on the authority of
according to.
to work carefully and conscientiously
"to take pains.
to adhere to (a promise), to follow or obey (a set of rules, procedures, etc.)
"to stick to.
"to endure, to be sufficient; to survive by resisting; to persist in one's efforts"
"to hold out. Comment: The first definition for to hold out has the opposite meaning of the second definition for to give out (seventh idiom, this lesson).
to stop functioning; to stop burning; to leave home or work (also: to step out)
"to go out.
to get excited, angry, or flustered
"to lose one's cool.
to tolerate, to accept unwillingly
"to put up with.
to support, to back (also: to go along with); to understand or follow what someone is saying
"to be with (someone).
to choose, to select (S)
to pick out.
to remove, to extract (S); to go on a date with (S) (also: to go out with)
"to take out.
to lose, or cause to lose, moisture gradually (S); to stop drinking alcohol in excess (also: to sober up)
to dry out.
a minimum of, no fewer (or less) than
at least.
to pay the cost or bill
"to pick up the tab. Comment: This idiom applies when someone pays for the cost of another person's meal, tickets, etc.
to become worn gradually through use (also: to wear away, to wear through) (S)
"to wear down. Comment: Compare with to wear out (to become useless from wear) in Lesson 8.
to succeed
"to make good.
to outgrow, to become too old for, to be a result of
"to grow out of.
to make progress; to manage to live in a certain state of health
to get along.
"to receive one's just punishment (S)"
to serve (someone) right. Comment: This idiom is usually used at the beginning of a sentence after the subject it. Compare the following examples with those in the previous idiom above.
to become sick, well, tired, busy, wet, etc.
to get sick (well, tired, busy, wet, etc.). Comment: This idiom consists of a combination of get and various adjectives.
to become involved or concerned with something; to enter a place for a brief time (also: to step into)
to step in.
to punish with full penalty, to be harsh on
to throw the book at.
to fulfill a promise, to be responsible
"to keep one's word. Comment: An idiom with the opposite meaning is to break one's word.
to leave or to descend from a vehicle
"to get out of/to get off. Comment: To get out of is used for cars; to get off is used for all other forms of transportation.
to surrender, to stop resisting
"to give in.
to become lost; to go away in order not to bother
to get lost. Comment: The second definition provides a very informal, even rude, meaning that should be used only with close friends. It is sometimes used in a joking manner.
to stop doing as a regular activity, to remove oneself from a situation
"to bow out. Comment: The related idiom to want out indicates that someone desires to bow out.
by a great margin, clearly
"by far.
to register at a hotel or motel; to leave or deposit for transporting or safekeeping (S)
to check in. Comment: The adjective form check-in derives from this idiom.
to work with the purpose of fulfilling a requirement or being equal to others
to catch up. Comment: The idiom is often followed by the preposition with and a noun phrase. It is similar in meaning to keep up with from Lesson 17.
to quarrel with, to confront
to have it out with.
to enter or to board a vehicle
"to get in/to get on. Comment: To get in is used for cars; to get on is used for all other forms of transportation.
to desire greatly, to be determined to
"to have one's heart set on.
not modern, not current, not timely; no longer available in published form
"out of date. Comment: Again, hyphens separate the parts of this idiom when it precedes a noun form as in the second example. The passive verb to be outdated derives from this idiom.
to submit or deliver something that is due (S)
to hand in.
to give the same name as another (S)
"to name after.
to visit in order to enjoy (S); to decrease the size of clothes (S); to deceive, to fool (S)
to take in.
to write a note to someone (S)
"to drop (someone) a line.
mainly, especially
"above all.
to end, to stop
"to come to an end. Comment: This idiom is used with finally and never when some activity lasts too long.
to assemble (S)
"to put together. Comment: A noun or pronoun usually divides this idiom. The preposition back is used when something has been disassembled and then is being reassembled, as in the second example.
to read or see clearly (S); to prepare a legal document, such as a will, a check, etc. (S)
"to make out.
to place oneself in a flat position, to recline
"to lie down.
be accustomed to
"to be used to. Comment: This idiom refers to a situation, action, or habit that continues in the present. The idiom is always followed by a noun or gerund phrase.
to remain calm during an emergency
"to keep one's head.
to recover safely from an unpleasant or dangerous situation
"to land on one's feet.
until now, until the present time (also: up to now, as of yet)
so far. Comment: This idiom is usually used with the present perfect tense.
to discuss informally (over a period of time) (S) (also: to toss around)
to kick (something) around.
to handle a difficult situation with determination
to take the bull by the horns. Comment: This idiom is usually used when someone has been postponing an action for some time and finally wants or needs to resolve it.
to leave the ground (for airplanes); to leave, often in a hurry
to take off. Comment: The noun form takeoff derives from this idiom.
to reduce, to lessen (also: to cut back on)
to cut down on.
to distribute in large quantity (S); to speak of others in a critical manner (S)
"to dish out.
to be able to do something special; to arrive at a place, such as home, work, etc.
"to get to. Comment: For the second definition, do not use the preposition to with the words home or there.
to terminate, to discontinue (S)
to break off.
just after, when
"as soon as.
"to show, to indicate, to bring to one's attention (S)"
to point out.
"to use well, to profit from; to use another person's weaknesses to gain what one wants"
"to take advantage of.
to be known without the need to mention
"to go without saying. Comment: This idiom occurs with a that-clause, often with the pronoun it as the subject.
to be careful or cautious (also: to watch out)
"to look out. Comment: Both of these idioms can occur with the preposition for.
to do the best that one can in a poor situation
"to make the best of.
to consider carefully before deciding (S)
"to think over.
to alternate, to change people while doing something
to take turns (doing something).
to cause to happen
to bring about. Comment: This idiom is used to indicate who or what caused something to come about.
to be wearing (S)
to have on.
to examine, to inspect closely (also: to go over, to read over, to check over) (S)
to look over. Comment: Go over is different from the other forms because it is not separable.
to remove by cutting (S); to stop doing something (S) (for the second definition, also: to knock it off)
"to cut out. Comment: For the second definition, the idiom is usually separated by the pronoun it.
to create a disturbance, usually by complaining
to make waves. Comment: This idiom is similar in meaning to the previous idiom, but the emphasis is on the aspect of complaining rather than causing embarrassment.
to become or result; to appear, to attend (also: to come out)
to turn out. Comment: The noun form turnout derives from the second definition of the idiom.
to arise, to rise from a bed; to make someone arise (S)
"to get up. Comment: For the last definition a noun phrase must separate the verb and particle.
to make very weary due to difficult conditions or hard effort (also: to wear out) (S)
to tire out.
to return a bought or borrowed item (also: to take back) (S)
"to bring back. Comment: To bring back is used when you are speaking at the place that an item is bought or borrowed; To take back is used when speaking at another place.
to be sold at a certain price; to seek or strive for
"to go for.
every second (one), alternate (ones)
every other (one).
finally, after a long time
"at last.
to go, or cause to go, more slowly (also: to slow up) (S)
"to slow down. Comment: This idiom can be used both with and without an object.
to become widespread suddenly
to break out.
to accomplish, to execute (S) (also: to go through with)
"to carry out.
to require; to request, to urge
"to call for.
to suppress, to quell (S); to criticize unfairly (S)
"to put down.
modern, current, timely
up to date. Comment: Hyphens (-) separate the parts of this idiom when it precedes a noun form, as in the third example. The verb to update derives from this idiom.
to cheat, to take unfair advantage of (also: to rip off, the ripoff)
"to stick it to somebody.
to disassemble, to separate the parts of something (S)
to take apart. Comment: A noun or pronoun usually divides this idiom.
formerly did, had the habit of
used to. Comment: This idiom is used to indicate a past situation, action, or habit that does not exist in the present. The idiom is always followed by a simple verb form.
to introduce an unexpected topic, causing embarrassment (S)
to throw (someone) a curve.
to revise, to do again (S)
"to do over. Comment: A noun or pronoun must separate the two parts of this idiom.
to say or do the wrong thing
"to put one's foot in. Comment: This idiom is used with the noun phrase one's mouth or the pronoun it.
to be at the moment of doing something, to be ready
"to be about to do something. Comment: This idiom is often used with the adverb just.
to know about, to be familiar with; to consider
"to hear of. Comment: The second definition is always used in the negative.
To be or become distracted, preoccupied, or unfocused from the present moment or the task at hand. Example: I love looking out the window on the train and just ___ for a few minutes.
space out
in a backward and forward motion
back and forth.
to finish, to complete
"to get through doing something, to get through with something. Comment: This idiom is followed either by the -ing form of a verb (a gerund) or by the preposition with.
to accept what one says as true, to believe
"to take somebody at one's word.
to point or place upwards (S); to rob (S)
"to stick up.
to discard (S); to remove by force (S); to refuse to consider, to reject (S)
to throw out.
rarely, infrequently
once in a blue moon.
permanently, forever
"for good.
to refuse to consider, to prohibit (S)
"to rule out.
